concepts.title: Differentiability
concepts.conceptBody:
Differentiation

The process of finding derivatives is called differentiation. So what is derivative?

Loosely speaking derivative is a measure of how a function changes as its input changes.

The derivative of a function /differentiation is also called the differential coefficient (d.c.) of $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$.

Derivative $=\text{li}{{\text{m}}_{h\to 0}}\frac{\text{ change }\text{ in }y}{\text{ change }\text{ in }x}$

i.e. the rate of change of $\text{y}$ with respect to the change in value of $\text{x}$.

Some notations of derivatives are

${f}',{f}'\left( x \right),\frac{dy}{dx},\frac{d}{dx}f\left( x \right)$

The derivative of the function $f$ with respect to the variable $x$ is the function ${f}'$ whose value of $\text{x}$ is

${f}'\left( x \right)=\underset{h\to 0}{\mathop{\text{lim}}}\,\frac{f\left( x+h \right)-f\left( x \right)}{h}\text{ provided }\text{ that }\text{ limit }\text{ exists}\text{. }$

Differentiability 

Before going to find the derivatives of some functions we need to check whether they are differentiable or not.

For this we need to prove that R.H.L. (Right Hand Limit) & L.H.L. (Left Hand Limit) exists are and equal.

To find R.H.K. & L.H.L. at a point $\text{x}=\text{a}$.

R.H.L.

$\underset{h\to 0}{\mathop{\text{lim}}}\,\frac{f\left( a+h \right)-f\left( a \right)}{\left( a+h \right)-h}=\underset{h\to 0}{\mathop{\text{lim}}}\,\frac{f\left( a+h \right)-f\left( a \right)}{h}$

L.H.L.

$\underset{h\to 0}{\mathop{\text{lim}}}\,\frac{f\left( a-h \right)-f\left( a \right)}{\left( a-h \right)-a}=\underset{h\to 0}{\mathop{\text{lim}}}\,\frac{f\left( a-h \right)-f\left( a \right)}{-h}$

for a function to be differentiable at $x=a$. 

R.H.L. = L.H.L. = k (a finite number)

concepts.title: When does derivative not exist
concepts.conceptBody:
When does a Function not have a Derivative at a Point? 

A function has a derivative at a point ${{x}_{0}}$ if the slope of the tangent lines from left hand side is equal to slope of tangent from Right hand side and attains a finite value.

Case-I. 

At ${{\text{P}}_{1}}$

Slope of tangent from LHS = slope of tangent from RHS

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ function is differentiable at $\text{x}=\text{a}$

Case-II 

At ${{\text{P}}_{2}}$

Slope of tangent from LHS $\ne $ Slope of tangent from RHS

but $\text{LHL}=\text{RHL}$

Therefore, function is continuous but non-differentiable.

Case-III 

At ${{\text{P}}_{3}}$

Slope of tangent from LHL $\to -\infty $

Slope of tangent from LHL $\to \infty $

but $\text{LHL}=\text{RHL}$

Therefore, function is continuous & non-differentiable.

Case-IV 

At ${{\text{P}}_{4}}$

Its vertical tangent

Slope of tangent from LHS $=$ Slope of tangent from RHS

but slope $\to \infty $ or $-\infty $

Therefore, its not differentiable.

Case-V 

At ${{\text{P}}_{5}}$

Discontinuity

Therefore, its neither continuous nor differentiable

Therefore, we conclude for a function to be differentiable, it must be continuous but vice-versa is not true.

concepts.title: First Principle
concepts.conceptBody:
Calculating Derivatives using first principle 

Steps for Calculating $\text{{f}'}\left( \text{x} \right)$ 

Using first principle

  1. Write expression for $f\left( x \right)$ and $f\left( x+h \right)$
  2. Expand and simplify the difference quotient

$\frac{f\left( x+h \right)-f\left( x \right)}{h}$

3 .Using the simplified quotient, find ${f}'\left( x \right)$ by evaluating the limit

${f}'\left( x \right)=\underset{h\to 0}{\mathop{\text{lim}}}\,\frac{f\left( x+h \right)-f\left( x \right)}{h}$

 

Q) (a) Differentiate $f\left( x \right)=\frac{x}{x-1}$, 

(b) Where does the curve $y=f\left( x \right)$ have slope -1? 

Solution: 

We take the three steps listed above

Step-1: Here we have $f\left( x \right)=\frac{x}{x-1}$

and

Step-2. $\frac{f\left( x+h \right)-f\left( x \right)}{h}=\frac{\frac{x+h}{x+h-1}-\frac{x}{x-1}}{h}$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & ~=\frac{1}{h}\cdot \frac{\left( \text{x}+\text{h} \right)\left( \text{x}-1 \right)-\text{x}\left( \text{x}+\text{h}-1 \right)}{\left( \text{x}+\text{h}-1 \right)\left( \text{x}-1 \right)} \\ {} & ~=\frac{1}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ h}}\cdot \frac{-\text{h}}{\left( \text{x}+\text{h}-1 \right)\left( \text{x}-1 \right)},\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ and }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \end{array}$

Step-3. 

${f}'\left( x \right)=\underset{h\to 0}{\mathop{\text{lim}}}\,\frac{-1}{\left( x+h-1 \right)\left( x-1 \right)}=\frac{-1}{{{(x-1)}^{2}}}$

(b) The slope of $y=f\left( x \right)$ will be -1 provided

$-\frac{1}{{{(x-1)}^{2}}}=-1$

This equation is equivalent to ${{(\text{x}-1)}^{2}}=1$, so $\text{x}=2$ or $\text{x}=0$.

concepts.title: Derivatives
concepts.conceptBody:
Derivatives of basic functions

  1. $\frac{\text{d}}{\text{dx}}\left( \text{c} \right)=0$, where $\text{c}$ is a constant.
  2. $\frac{d}{dx}{{x}^{n}}=n\cdot {{x}^{n-1}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\And \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{d}{dx}{{x}^{-n}}=-n{{x}^{-n-1}}=\frac{-n}{{{x}^{n+1}}}$
  3. $\frac{d}{dx}\left( {{e}^{x}} \right)={{e}^{x}}$
  4. $\frac{d}{dx}\left( {{a}^{x}} \right)={{a}^{x}}\text{log}a$
  5. $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{d}{dx}\left( \text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{e}}x \right)=\frac{1}{x}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\And \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{d}{dx}\left( \text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{a}}x \right)=\frac{1}{x\text{lo}{{\text{g}}_{e}}a}$
  6. $\frac{d}{dx}\left( \text{sin}x \right)=\text{cos}x,\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{d}{dx}\left( \text{cos}x \right)=-\text{sin}x$
  7. $\frac{d}{d x}(\tan x)=\sec ^2 x \quad, \quad \frac{d}{d x}(\cot x)=-\operatorname{cosec}^2 x$
  8. $\frac{d}{d x}(\sec x)=\sec x \tan x \quad, \quad \frac{d}{d x}(\operatorname{cosec} x)=-\operatorname{cosec} x \cot x$
  9. $\frac{d}{dx}\left( \text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}x \right)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-{{x}^{2}}}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ },\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{d}{dx}\left( \text{co}{{\text{s}}^{-1}}x \right)=\frac{-1}{\sqrt{1-{{x}^{2}}}}$ for $\{-1<x<1\}$
  10. $\frac{d}{dx}\left( \text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}x \right)=\frac{1}{1+{{x}^{2}}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ },\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{d}{dx}\left( \text{co}{{\text{t}}^{-1}}x \right)=-\frac{1}{1+{{x}^{2}}}$ for $x\in \mathbf{R}$
  11. $\frac{d}{dx}\left( \text{se}{{\text{c}}^{-1}}x \right)=\frac{1}{\left| x \right|\sqrt{{{x}^{2}}-1}},\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{d}{dx}\left( \text{cose}{{\text{c}}^{-1}}x \right)=\frac{-1}{\left| x \right|\sqrt{{{x}^{2}}-1}}$ for $\left| x \right|>1$

concepts.title: Rules of Differentiation
concepts.conceptBody:
Rules of differentiation 

Powers, Multiples, Sums and Differences 

The first rule of differentiation is that the derivative of every constant function is zero.

Rule-1. Derivative of a Constant 

If $\text{c}$ is constant, then $\frac{\text{d}}{\text{dx}}\text{c}=0$.

Illustration. $\frac{\text{d}}{\text{dx}}\left( 10 \right)=0,\frac{\text{d}}{\text{dx}}\left( \sqrt{2} \right)=0$

The rule $\left( d/dx \right)\left( c \right)=0$ is another way to say that the values of constant functions never change and that the slope of a horizontal line is zero at every point.

Rule-2. Power Rule for Positive Integers 

If $\text{n}$ is a positive, then

$\frac{d}{dx}{{x}^{n}}=n{{x}^{n-1}}$

To apply the Power Rule, we subtract 1 from the origin exponent (n) and multiply the result by $n$.

Illustration: 

The Power Rule for Negative Integers 

The power Rule for negative integers is the same as the rule for positive integers.

Power Rule for Negative Integers 

If $\text{n}$ is a negative integer and $\text{x}\ne 0$, then

$\frac{d}{dx}\left( {{x}^{n}} \right)=n{{x}^{n-1}}$

Illustration: $\frac{\text{d}}{\text{dx}}\left( \frac{1}{\text{x}} \right)=\frac{\text{d}}{\text{dx}}\left( {{\text{x}}^{-1}} \right)=\left( -1 \right){{\text{x}}^{-2}}=-\frac{1}{{{\text{x}}^{2}}}$

$\frac{d}{dx}\left( \frac{4}{{{x}^{3}}} \right)=4\frac{d}{dx}\left( {{x}^{-1}} \right)=4\left( -3 \right){{x}^{-4}}=-\frac{12}{{{x}^{4}}}$

 

Q) Find an equation for the tangent to the curve $y=x+\frac{2}{x}$. 

Solution: 

The slope of the curve is

$\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{d}{dx}\left( x \right)+2\frac{d}{dx}\left( \frac{1}{x} \right)=1+2\left( -\frac{1}{{{x}^{2}}} \right)=1-\frac{2}{{{x}^{2}}}$

The slope at $\text{x}=1$ is

${{\left. \frac{\text{dy}}{\text{dx}} \right|}_{\text{x}=1}}={{\left[ 1-\frac{2}{{{\text{x}}^{2}}} \right]}_{\text{x}=1}}=1-2=1.$

The line through $\left( 1,3 \right)$ with slope $\text{m}=-1$ is

$y-3=(-1)(x-1)$ Point-slope equation

$\mathrm{y}=-\mathrm{x}+1+3$

$y=-x+4$

Rule-3. The Constant Multiple Rule 

If $\text{u}$ is a differentiable function of $\text{x}$, and $\text{c}$ is a constant, then

$\frac{d}{dx}\left( cu \right)=c\frac{du}{dx}$

In particular, if $\text{n}$ is a positive integer, then

$\frac{d}{dx}\left( c{{x}^{n}} \right)=cn{{x}^{n-1}}$

Illustration: $\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(-4 \mathrm{x}^3\right)=-4.3 \mathrm{x}^2=-12 \mathrm{x}^2$

says that if we rescale the graph of $y={{x}^{2}}$ multiplying each y-coordinate by 3, then we multiply the slope at each point by 3.

Rule 4. The Sum Rule 

If $\text{u}$ and $\text{v}$ are differentiable functions of $\text{x}$, then their sum $\text{u}+\text{v}$ is differentiable at every point where $\text{u}$ and $\text{v}$ are both differentiable. At such points

$\frac{d}{dx}\left( u+v \right)=\frac{du}{dx}+\frac{dv}{dx}$

Combining the Sum Rule with the Constant Multiple Rule gives the equivalent Difference Rule, which says that the derivative of a difference of differentiable functions is the difference of their derivatives

$\frac{d}{dx}\left( u-v \right)=\frac{d}{dx}\left[ u+\left( -1 \right)v \right]=\frac{du}{dx}+\left( -1 \right)\frac{du}{dx}=\frac{du}{dx}-\frac{dv}{dx}$

The Sum Rule also extends to sums of more than two functions, as long as there are only finitely many functions in the sum. If ${{\text{u}}_{1}},{{\text{u}}_{2}},\ldots ,{{\text{u}}_{\text{n}}}$ are differentiable at $\text{x}$, then so is ${{\text{u}}_{1}}+{{\text{u}}_{2}}+\ldots +{{\text{u}}_{\text{n}}}$, and

$\frac{d}{dx}\left( {{u}_{1}}+{{u}_{2}}+\ldots +{{u}_{n}} \right)=\frac{d{{u}_{1}}}{dx}+\frac{d{{u}_{2}}}{dx}+\ldots +\frac{d{{u}_{n}}}{dx}$

Illustration: 

(a)$y=5 x^4+12 x^2$ (b)$y=x^3+\frac{4}{3} x^2-5 x+1$

$\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\mathrm{x}^4\right)+\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(12 \mathrm{x}^2\right)$

$\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \mathrm{x}^3+\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\frac{4}{3} \mathrm{x}^2\right)-\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(5 \mathrm{x})+\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(1)$

$=20 x^3+24 x$

$=3 \mathrm{x}^2+\frac{4}{3} \cdot 2 \mathrm{x}-5+0$

$=3 x^2+\frac{8}{3} x-5$

 

Q) Does the curve $y={{x}^{4}}-2{{x}^{2}}+2$ have any horizontal tangents? If so, where? 

Solution: 

The horizontal tangents, if any, occur where the slope $\text{dy}/\text{dx}$ is zero. To find these points, we

  1. Calculate $\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{d}{dx}\left( {{x}^{4}}-2{{x}^{2}}+2 \right)=4{{x}^{3}}-4x$
  2. Solve the equation $\frac{dy}{dx}=0$ for $x$;

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & 4{{x}^{3}}-4x=0 \\ {} & 4x\left( {{x}^{2}}-1 \right)=0 \\ {} & x=0,1,-1 \\ \end{array}$

The curve $y={{x}^{4}}-2{{x}^{2}}+2$ has horizontal tangent at $x=0,1$ and -1. The corresponding points on the curve are $\left( 0,2 \right),\left( 1,1 \right)$ and $\left( -1,1 \right)$.

Rule 5 The product Rule 

If $\text{u}$ and $\text{v}$ differentiable at $\text{x}$, then so is their product $\text{uv}$, and

$\frac{d}{dx}\left( uv \right)=u\frac{dv}{dx}+v\frac{du}{dx}$

Q) Find the derivative of $y=\left( {{x}^{2}}+1 \right)\left( {{x}^{3}}+3 \right)$. 

Solution: 

From the Product Rule with $\text{u}={{\text{x}}^{2}}+1$ and $\text{v}={{\text{x}}^{3}}+3$.

We find

$\frac{d}{d x}\left[\left(x^2+1\right)\left(x^3+3\right)\right]=\left(x^2+1\right)\left(3 x^2\right)+\left(x^3+3\right)(2 x)$

$=3 x^4+3 x^2+2 x^4+6 x$

$=5 x^4+3 x^2+6 x$

Above Illustration be done as well (perhaps better) by multiplying out the original expression for $\text{y}$ and differentiating the resulting polynomial. We now check:

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & y=\left( {{x}^{2}}+1 \right)\left( {{x}^{3}}+3 \right)={{x}^{5}}+{{x}^{3}}+3{{x}^{2}}+3 \\ {} & \frac{dy}{dx}=5{{x}^{4}}+3{{x}^{2}}+6x \\ \end{array}$

 

Q) Let $y=uv$ be the product of the function $u$ and $v$. Find ${y}'\left( 2 \right)$ if 

$\text{u}\left( 2 \right)=3,\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ {u}'}\left( 2 \right)=-4,\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ v}\left( 2 \right)=1,\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ and }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ {v}'}\left( 2 \right)=2$ 

Solution: 

From the Product Rule, in the form

$\text{{y}'}={{(\text{uv})}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}}=\text{u}{{\text{v}}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}}+\text{u}{{\text{v}}^{\text{ }\!\!'\!\!\text{ }}}$

we have

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \text{{y}'}\left( 2 \right)=\text{u}\left( 2 \right)\text{{v}'}\left( 2 \right)+\text{v}\left( 2 \right)\text{{u}'}\left( 2 \right) \\ {} & ~=\left( 3 \right)\left( 2 \right)+\left( 1 \right)\left( -4 \right)=6-4=2. \\ \end{array}$

Rule-6. The Quotient Rule 

If $\text{u}$ and $\text{v}$ are differentiable at $\text{x}$, and $\text{v}\left( \text{x} \right)\ne 0$, then the quotient $\text{u}/\text{v}$ is differentiable at $\text{x}$, and

$\frac{d}{dx}\left( \frac{u}{v} \right)=\frac{v\frac{du}{dx}-u\frac{dv}{dx}}{{{v}^{2}}}$
